The wars of nations appear significant in history, but we would not have a record of this war if Abram and Lot had not been involved. Out of greed, Lot settled in fertile, but wicked Sodom. Its inhabitants were the most deserving of vengeance among all the descendants of Canaan. The invaders came from Chaldea and Persia, then only small kingdoms. They took Lot along with the rest, and his possessions. Though he was righteous and Abram's brother’s son, yet he was involved in this trouble with the others. Neither our own righteousness nor our relationship to those favored by Heaven will be our security when God’s judgments are abroad. Many an honest person suffers because of their wicked neighbors; it is wise to separate ourselves from them, or at least to distinguish ourselves. A relative as close as Lot should have been a companion and a disciple of Abram. If he chose to live in Sodom, he must accept responsibility for sharing in Sodom’s losses. When we deviate from our duty, we put ourselves outside God's protection and cannot expect that the choices made by our desires will bring us comfort. They took Lot’s possessions; it is just with God to deprive us of pleasures when we allow ourselves to be deprived of the pleasure of him.

Abram takes this opportunity to give a true demonstration of his genuine friendship for Lot. We should be ready to help those in distress, especially relatives and friends. And though others may have failed in their duty to us, we must not neglect our duty to them. Abram rescued the captives. As we have opportunity, we must do good to all.

Melchizedek is mentioned as a king of Salem, thought to be the place later called Jerusalem, and it is generally believed that he was only a man. The apostle’s words state only that sacred history says nothing about his ancestors. This silence in Scripture is meant to raise our thoughts to Him whose generation cannot be declared. Bread and wine were suitable refreshment for Abram's weary followers; and it is remarkable that Christ appointed the same as memorials of his body and blood, which are truly nourishment for the soul. Melchizedek blessed Abram from God. He blessed God from Abram. We should give thanks for other people’s mercies as if they were our own. Jesus Christ, our great High Priest, is the Mediator both of our prayers and praises, and not only offers up ours, but his own for us. Abram gave him the tenth of the spoils. When we have received some great mercy from God, it is fitting that we express our thankfulness through a special act of pious charity. Jesus Christ, our great Melchizedek, deserves to be honored and humbly acknowledged as our King and Priest; not only the tithe of all, but everything we have, must be given up to him.

Observe the king of Sodom’s grateful offer to Abram: Give me the souls, and take you the substance. Gratitude teaches us to recompense to the utmost of our power those who have undergone hardships, run risks, and spent resources for our service and benefit. Abram generously refused this offer. He accompanied his refusal with a good reason, Lest you should say, I made Abram rich; which would reflect upon God’s promise and covenant, as if He would not have enriched Abraham without the spoils of Sodom. The people of God must, for their reputation's sake, take care to avoid doing anything that looks petty or mercenary, or that suggests covetousness and self-seeking. Abram can trust the Possessor of Heaven and earth to provide for him.
